page: Alibaba Cloud ecs.hfr8i.xlarge is a memory-optimized virtual machine instance powered by the KVM hypervisor. It features 4 dedicated vCPUs mapped to 4 physical cores with 1 thread per core, utilizing an Intel Xeon 6462C processor running at a clock speed of 3.3 GHz on the x86_64 architecture. The instance is equipped with 32.0 GB of system memory, providing a high resource density of 8.0 GB of RAM per core.

This instance does not include local storage or integrated GPU hardware, meaning storage requirements must be met through external network-attached block storage. It provides a baseline network bandwidth of 4 Gbps and does not include complimentary public IPv4 addresses. The dedicated CPU allocation ensures that compute resources are not shared with other tenants, offering predictable processing capabilities.

Given its high memory-to-vCPU ratio of 1:8, the ecs.hfr8i.xlarge is designed for memory-intensive workloads that do not require massive parallel compute or local disk storage. Suitable applications include in-memory databases, caching layers, enterprise applications, and lightweight data processing tasks where memory capacity is the primary constraint.
description: Alibaba Cloud ecs.hfr8i.xlarge is a memory-optimized virtual machine instance running on the KVM hypervisor. It is equipped with 4 dedicated vCPUs powered by an Intel Xeon 6462C processor operating at 3.3 GHz on the x86_64 architecture. With 32.0 GB of RAM, the instance delivers a high memory-to-core ratio of 8.0 GB per core, making it suitable for memory-intensive applications. The instance features a dedicated CPU allocation with 1 thread per physical core, ensuring consistent compute performance. It does not include local storage or GPU accelerators, relying instead on network-attached storage options, and provides a baseline network bandwidth of 4 Gbps. This hardware profile is well-suited for workloads such as in-memory databases, caching layers, and enterprise applications requiring high memory density relative to compute capacity.
